title: Benefits of preparing for childbirth with mindfulness training: a randomized controlled trial with active comparison
headline: Benefits of preparing for childbirth with mindfulness training: a randomized controlled trial with active comp
summary: A randomized controlled trial of mindfulness / meditation examined stress & anxiety. This study suggests mindfulness training carefully tailored to address fear and pain of childbirth may lead to important maternal mental health benefits, including improvements in childbirth-related appraisals ﻿and﻿ the prevention of postpartum depression symptoms. There is also some indication that MIL participants may use mindfulness coping in lieu of systemic opioid pain medication.
keyFindings: Mindfulness approaches, now widely disseminated, can alleviate symptoms of both chronic and acute pain and improve psychological adjustment, suggesting potential benefit when applied to childbirth education.

This study suggests mindfulness training carefully tailored to address fear and pain of childbirth may lead to important maternal mental health benefits, including improvements in childbirth-related appraisals ﻿and﻿ the prevention of postpartum depression symptoms.
There is also some indication that MIL participants may use mindfulness coping in lieu of systemic opioid pain medication.
limitations: This study﻿, the Prenatal Education About Reducing Labor Stress (PEARLS) study, is a randomized controlled trial (RCT; n = 30) of a short, time-intensive, 2.5-day mindfulness-based childbirth preparation course offered as a weekend workshop﻿, the Mind in Labor (MIL): Working with Pain in Childbirth, based on Mindfulness-Based Childbirth and Parenting (MBCP) education.﻿ First-time mothers in the late 3rd trimester of pregnancy were randomized to attend either the MIL course or a standard childbirth preparation course with no mind-body focus.
Participants completed self-report assessments pre-intervention, post-intervention, and post-birth, and medical record data were collected.
Single-trial result; requires replication, and self-reported outcomes may inflate apparent effects.
The practice studied (mindfulness / meditation) is not the Miracle of Mind practice; findings do not transfer directly to MoM.
